Parliamentary elections were held in [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") on 3 April 2022 to elect the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Hungary) "National Assembly (Hungary)"), coinciding with [a referendum](/wiki/2022_Hungarian_LGBT_in_education_referendum "2022 Hungarian LGBT in education referendum").[[1]](#cite_note-4)[[2]](#cite_note-5) Hungary's incumbent prime minister [Viktor Orbán](/wiki/Viktor_Orb%C3%A1n "Viktor Orbán") won re-election to a fourth term. Addressing his supporters after the partial results showed [Fidesz-KDNP](/wiki/Fidesz-KDNP "Fidesz-KDNP") leading by a wide margin, Orbán said: "We won a victory so big that you can see it from the moon, and you can certainly see it from Brussels."[[3]](#cite_note-:0-6) Opposition leader [Péter Márki-Zay](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_M%C3%A1rki-Zay "Péter Márki-Zay") admitted defeat shortly after Orbán's speech.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-7) [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters") described it as a "crushing victory".[[5]](#cite_note-:2-8)

With 54.13% of the popular vote, Fidesz received the highest vote share by any party since the [Fall of Communism](/wiki/Fall_of_Communism "Fall of Communism") in 1989. The election had been predicted to be closer than in previous years but Fidesz still held a 5–6 percentage point lead in the polls leading up to the vote.[[6]](#cite_note-:4-9) [OSCE](/wiki/OSCE "OSCE") deployed a full monitoring mission for the vote.[[7]](#cite_note-:5-10) The results showed that Fidesz outperformed polls, winning its first absolute majority of the vote share since [2010](/wiki/2010_Hungarian_parliamentary_election "2010 Hungarian parliamentary election") while expanding its supermajority to control 135 seats of the 199-seat Parliament, comfortably ahead of the opposition alliance [United for Hungary](/wiki/United_for_Hungary "United for Hungary"), which was set to win 57 seats after 100% of the votes had been counted.[[8]](#cite_note-:3-11) The [Our Homeland Movement](/wiki/Our_Homeland_Movement "Our Homeland Movement") party won seats for the first time, obtaining 6 seats.[[9]](#cite_note-hungarytoday.hu-12)

Internationally, the election was seen as a victory for [right-wing populism](/wiki/Right-wing_populism "Right-wing populism") with Orbán being an ally of then former US President [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") and other right-wing figures.[[10]](#cite_note-NBC_News-13)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

### Creation of joint opposition list

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Creation of joint opposition list")]

On 8 April 2018, just like in 2010 and 2014, [Fidesz-KDNP](/wiki/Fidesz-KDNP "Fidesz-KDNP") won with two-third majority, collecting almost every second votes of people. Following the election, the main opposition parties, which had previously run alone or partly united, gradually realized that Fidesz could only be defeated through full cooperation. The first step of this was a joint demonstration in December of that year against the so called "slave law", which would have required workers to work in overtime.[[11]](#cite_note-14)

In [2019 local elections](/wiki/2019_Hungarian_local_elections "2019 Hungarian local elections"), [Democratic Coalition](/wiki/Democratic_Coalition_(Hungary) "Democratic Coalition (Hungary)"), [Jobbik](/wiki/Jobbik "Jobbik"), [LMP – Hungary's Green Party](/wiki/LMP_%E2%80%93_Hungary%27s_Green_Party "LMP – Hungary's Green Party"), [Hungarian Socialist Party](/wiki/Hungarian_Socialist_Party "Hungarian Socialist Party"), [Momentum Movement](/wiki/Momentum_Movement "Momentum Movement"), [Dialogue for Hungary](/wiki/Dialogue_for_Hungary "Dialogue for Hungary"), and numerous other parties or [independent candidates](/wiki/Independent_candidates "Independent candidates") ran in joint lists. It enabled opposition parties to win the majority in the [General Assembly of Budapest](/wiki/General_Assembly_of_Budapest "General Assembly of Budapest") and in ten of the 23 cities with county rights.[[12]](#cite_note-15)

On 13 August 2020, the six parties decided to field joint candidates in all of Hungary's 106 constituencies and launch a joint program for the elections,[[13]](#cite_note-16) later agreed on running with a common candidate for [Prime Minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Hungary "Prime Minister of Hungary"), to be selected in a primary election. They expected the candidate to be selected by 23 October 2021.[[14]](#cite_note-17) On 20 December 2020, DK, Jobbik, Momentum, MSZP, LMP and Dialogue decided on running together on a [common list](/wiki/United_for_Hungary "United for Hungary"), solidifying their electoral alliance.[[15]](#cite_note-18)[[16]](#cite_note-19) The leaders of the respective parties have also signed a declaration containing the terms of their cooperation, titled "Korszakváltás Garanciái" (Principles for Hungary's governance after 2022"). The opposition coalition was very ideologically diverse, something described by observers as a potential hindrance to presenting a united front to defeat Orbán.[[17]](#cite_note-20)

### Amendment of the electoral law

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Amendment of the electoral law")]

The electoral law, under which the previous two elections were held ([2014](/wiki/2014_Hungarian_parliamentary_election "2014 Hungarian parliamentary election") and [2018](/wiki/2018_Hungarian_parliamentary_election "2018 Hungarian parliamentary election")), specified the number of constituency candidates a party needed to run nationwide at 27, in at least 9 counties. On 15 December 2020, the National Assembly, in which the governing parties held the needed a two-third majority, voted to increase the requirements to a total of 71 constituency candidates in at least 14 counties and the [capital](/wiki/Budapest "Budapest").[[18]](#cite_note-21)[[19]](#cite_note-22)

### Opposition primary

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Opposition primary")]

Main article: [2021 Hungarian opposition primary](/wiki/2021_Hungarian_opposition_primary "2021 Hungarian opposition primary")

The [Opposition](/wiki/United_for_Hungary "United for Hungary") primary was held in [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary"), between 12 and 27 September 2021 (first round) and 10–16 October 2021 (second round), to select the candidate for Prime Minister supported by the opposition parties to form a coalition to compete in the 2022 Hungarian parliamentary election. It was the first countrywide primary election in the political history of Hungary. On 17 October 2021, non-partisan candidate [Péter Márki-Zay](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_M%C3%A1rki-Zay "Péter Márki-Zay") won in the runoff (second round) of the opposition primary, becoming the [United Opposition](/wiki/United_for_Hungary "United for Hungary") candidate in the election for prime minister. The parties had also selected common candidates for [single-member districts](/wiki/Single-member_district "Single-member district") via the primary in the first round.[[20]](#cite_note-23)

## Electoral system

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Electoral system")]

Main article: [Electoral system of Hungary](/wiki/Electoral_system_of_Hungary "Electoral system of Hungary")

The 199 members of the [National Assembly](/wiki/National_Assembly_(Hungary) "National Assembly (Hungary)") are to be elected by [mixed-member majoritarian representation](/wiki/Mixed-member_majoritarian_representation "Mixed-member majoritarian representation") with two methods; 106 are elected in single-member constituencies by [first-past-the-post voting](/wiki/First-past-the-post_voting "First-past-the-post voting"), with the remaining 93 elected from a single nationwide constituency mostly by [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation"), via a [partially compensatory system](/wiki/Scorporo "Scorporo") (a hybrid of [parallel voting](/wiki/Parallel_voting "Parallel voting") and [mixed single vote](/wiki/Mixed_single_vote "Mixed single vote")). The electoral threshold is set at 5%, although this is raised to 10% for coalitions of two parties and 15% for coalitions of three or more parties. Seats are allocated using the [d'Hondt method](/wiki/D%27Hondt_method "D'Hondt method").[[21]](#cite_note-24)

The governing party is allowed to draw the boundaries of the single-member constituencies, and the boundaries were gerrymandered by the Fidesz government prior to the 2014 elections. The redrawing was done in a way that smaller constituencies (with fewer than 70,000 voters) had higher concentrations of Fidesz voters than larger constituencies (over 80,000 voters), meaning fewer votes were required to elect Fidesz constituency candidates.[[22]](#cite_note-25) In 2010, seats varied in population by 35%.[[23]](#cite_note-26)

Since 2014 each of the Armenian, Bulgarian, Croatian, German, Greek, Polish, Romani, Romanian, Rusyn, Serbian, Slovak, Slovenian, and Ukrainian ethnic minorities can win one of the 93 party lists seats if they register as a specific list and reach a lowered quota of ![{\displaystyle {\frac {1}{4\times 93}}={\frac {1}{372}}\approx 0.27\%}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ba0c5aa024a240c5d5fa2237f0f2d68a4090a5d1) of the total of party list votes. Each minority is able to send a minority spokesman – without the rights of an MP – to the National Assembly, if the list does not reach this lowered quota.[[24]](#cite_note-27) Due to internal disputes, the National Self-Government of Romani did not participate in the election.[[25]](#cite_note-28)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Peace_and_Security_%281%29.png/250px-Peace_and_Security_%281%29.png)](/wiki/File:Peace_and_Security_(1).png)

Viktor Orbán celebrating his victory

Hungary's incumbent prime minister [Viktor Orbán](/wiki/Viktor_Orb%C3%A1n "Viktor Orbán"), seeking a fourth consecutive term in office, declared victory on Sunday night, with partial results showing his Fidesz party leading the vote by a wide margin. Addressing his supporters after the partial results, Orbán said: "We won a victory so big that you can see it from the moon, and you can certainly see it from Brussels."[[3]](#cite_note-:0-6) Orbán would go on to declare his victory as a rebuke against the Hungarian left, international media, [George Soros](/wiki/George_Soros "George Soros"), the "Brussels bureaucrats", and [Volodymyr Zelenskyy](/wiki/Volodymyr_Zelenskyy "Volodymyr Zelenskyy").[[29]](#cite_note-38)[[30]](#cite_note-39)[[31]](#cite_note-40) Opposition leader [Péter Márki-Zay](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_M%C3%A1rki-Zay "Péter Márki-Zay") admitted defeat shortly after Orbán's speech.[[4]](#cite_note-:1-7) [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters") described it as a "crushing victory", and that "voters endorsed his ambition of a conservative, 'illiberal' state and shrugged off concerns over Budapest's close ties with Moscow."[[5]](#cite_note-:2-8)

Although the election had been predicted to be closer than in previous years, Fidesz maintained a 5–6 percentage point lead in the polls leading up to the vote.[[6]](#cite_note-:4-9) The [Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe](/wiki/Organization_for_Security_and_Co-operation_in_Europe "Organization for Security and Co-operation in Europe") (OSCE) monitored the vote amid fears of [electoral fraud](/wiki/Electoral_fraud "Electoral fraud").[[7]](#cite_note-:5-10) Preliminary results showed Fidesz was set to outperform polls, retain its supermajority, and control 135 seats of the 199-seat Hungarian Parliament, significantly ahead of the main opposition alliance [United for Hungary](/wiki/United_for_Hungary "United for Hungary"), which was set to win 57 seats after 100% of the votes had been counted.[[8]](#cite_note-:3-11) The [Mi Hazánk](/wiki/Our_Homeland_Movement "Our Homeland Movement") party also entered Parliament for the first time, winning 6 seats.[[9]](#cite_note-hungarytoday.hu-12)

### Analysis

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Analysis")]

Domestically, the degree of Fidesz's victory was attributed to Fidesz's influence over the domestic media landscape, voters' desire for stability during the ongoing [Russian invasion of Ukraine](/wiki/Russian_invasion_of_Ukraine "Russian invasion of Ukraine"), strong ideological differences within the main opposition coalition, the perception of Márki-Zay as inexperienced, and [urban–rural political polarization](/wiki/Urban%E2%80%93rural_political_polarization "Urban–rural political polarization").[[32]](#cite_note-41) Some analysts claimed that the majority of [Jobbik](/wiki/Jobbik "Jobbik") voters turned out for Fidesz or Mi Hazánk instead of the united opposition.[[33]](#cite_note-42) Márki-Zay shared this assessment, admitting that the united opposition may have lost up to "two thirds" of Jobbik voters.[[34]](#cite_note-43)

On the Hungarian left, there was disagreement about how to interpret the united opposition’s defeat. DK leader [Ferenc Gyurcsány](/wiki/Ferenc_Gyurcs%C3%A1ny "Ferenc Gyurcsány") and Jobbik leader [Péter Jakab](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_Jakab "Péter Jakab") blamed Márky-Zay,[[35]](#cite_note-44)[[36]](#cite_note-45)[[37]](#cite_note-46) while [Bernadett Szél](/wiki/Bernadett_Sz%C3%A9l "Bernadett Szél") and [Ákos Hadházy](/wiki/%C3%81kos_Hadh%C3%A1zy "Ákos Hadházy") did not.[[38]](#cite_note-47) [Péter Ungár](/wiki/P%C3%A9ter_Ung%C3%A1r "Péter Ungár") stated that while it was a "strategic mistake" for a centre-left alliance to nominate the conservative Márky-Zay, the united opposition's "elitist" rhetoric may have hurt it with rural voters even in traditionally left-leaning constituencies.[[39]](#cite_note-48) Some opposition leaders even questioned whether forming a united front had been advantageous. [Imre Komjáthi](/wiki/Imre_Komj%C3%A1thi "Imre Komjáthi"), a [socialist](/wiki/Hungarian_Socialist_Party "Hungarian Socialist Party") MP, said that “the cracks and contradictions between our parties [in] ideology, technicalities, and resources had started to introduce coordination problems” during the election. He added that, in his view, the coalition had not been "genuine."[[40]](#cite_note-49)

*[Politico](/wiki/Politico "Politico")* predicted Orbán's victory would lead to further clashes between the Hungarian government and European Union institutions.[[41]](#cite_note-50) Analysts found that Fidesz performed strongest in areas with high birthrates[[42]](#cite_note-51) and with lower educational or economic attainment,[[43]](#cite_note-52)[[44]](#cite_note-53) while the united opposition did best in Budapest and in a few other urban areas.[[45]](#cite_note-54)

### Conduct

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Conduct")]

International observers' assessments of the electoral process were mixed. The OSCE monitoring mission evaluated that "the legal framework forms an adequate basis for democratic elections to be held, but a number of key aspects fall short of international standards." The OSCE added that the election day passed peacefully, with observers assessing the process as well-organized, orderly, and smooth. The monitoring mission further reported that the elections "offered voters distinct alternatives and were well run, but, while competitive, was marred by the pervasive overlapping of government and ruling coalition’s messaging that blurred the line between state and party, as well as by media bias and opaque campaign funding."[[46]](#cite_note-55) [Human Rights Watch](/wiki/Human_Rights_Watch "Human Rights Watch") stated that the election was "marred by serious concerns about its fairness", adding that the "EU has responded insufficiently to the hollowing out of democracy and the rule of law in Hungary",[[47]](#cite_note-56) while [Freedom House](/wiki/Freedom_House "Freedom House") described the election as neither free nor fair.[[48]](#cite_note-57)

### Anomalies over postal ballots

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Anomalies over postal ballots")]

Since the [2014 parliamentary election](/wiki/2014_Hungarian_parliamentary_election "2014 Hungarian parliamentary election"), the right to vote is extended to Hungarian citizens, after a registration process, who do not have a permanent residence in Hungary (i.e. mostly the [Hungarian diaspora](/wiki/Hungarian_diaspora "Hungarian diaspora") in the neighboring countries, who have dual citizenship); however, they can only vote for the national list of Hungarian parties, so they do not have a chance to vote in individual constituencies. Unlike those Hungarians who have a Hungarian address but live abroad, the members of the Hungarian diaspora can cast their ballots by post without having to travel to an embassy or consulate. Critics say this method do not provide sufficient guarantees to prevent election fraud. Since 2014, the ruling party Fidesz–KDNP usually acquired more than 90 percent of the vote among the Hungarian diaspora.[[49]](#cite_note-targumures-58)

Transtelex.ro, a Hungarian-language Romanian news portal revealed all possible frauds allowed by the legally undeveloped system. For instance, the registration process was not secure, as the data allowed a voter to register on behalf of another person and retrieve voting documents to any address. In the case of identification sheets, it was not possible to determine with absolute certainty whether it had actually been completed by the person whose name appears on it. As a result of a change in legislation in 2021, it was possible to return the vote without the posted security sealed envelope, therefore, there was no guarantee that the ballot paper had not been replaced.[[50]](#cite_note-59) There were several reports that a person could also easily vote on behalf of their family members. [Telex.hu](/wiki/Telex.hu "Telex.hu") reported a case that grandparents of a woman from [Odorheiu Secuiesc](/wiki/Odorheiu_Secuiesc "Odorheiu Secuiesc"), who resided in Budapest in the weeks of the election, filled her personal data and voted on her behalf. She said, "I have already been outraged that they have filled in the official paper and sent it for me, abusing my personal rights, but, in addition, they have voted for a party that I do not support because it represents an ideologically opposed position to me".[[51]](#cite_note-60)

According to reports, the activists of the Fidesz-ally [Alliance of Vojvodina Hungarians](/wiki/Alliance_of_Vojvodina_Hungarians "Alliance of Vojvodina Hungarians") (VMSZ) delivered the ballot papers instead of the [Pošta Srbije](/wiki/Po%C5%A1ta_Srbije "Pošta Srbije") in [Vojvodina](/wiki/Vojvodina "Vojvodina"), [Serbia](/wiki/Serbia "Serbia"). News portal *Szabad Magyar Szó* reported that the party officials sent messages to the registered voters to come to the local offices of the VMSZ to cast their ballot there.[[49]](#cite_note-targumures-58) In [Transylvania](/wiki/Transylvania "Transylvania"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), pro-Fidesz organizations and parties of the Hungarian minority, including the parliamentary party [Democratic Alliance of Hungarians in Romania](/wiki/Democratic_Alliance_of_Hungarians_in_Romania "Democratic Alliance of Hungarians in Romania") (UDMR or RMDSZ) also urged the registered voters to pass on their votes to them and not to entrust the postal service with the delivery of their ballot papers, because there is less chance that the vote will reach Hungary. In response, the [Poșta Română](/wiki/Po%C8%99ta_Rom%C3%A2n%C4%83 "Poșta Română") complained against the call.[[49]](#cite_note-targumures-58)

In the area between [Livezeni](/wiki/Livezeni "Livezeni") and [Târgu Mureș](/wiki/T%C3%A2rgu_Mure%C8%99 "Târgu Mureș") in Romania, a bag containing partially burnt Hungarian election postal ballot papers was found on illegal waste dump on 1 April 2022. Every ballot paper was filled out with votes for the opposition United for Hungary alliance or Our Homeland Movement.[[52]](#cite_note-61) The National Election Office (NVI) and the [Hungarian Civil Liberties Union](/wiki/Hungarian_Civil_Liberties_Union "Hungarian Civil Liberties Union") (TASZ) filed a criminal complaint over the burnt ballot papers to the [Hungarian Police](/wiki/Hungarian_Police "Hungarian Police"). Péter Márki-Zay, prime minister-candidate of the United for Hungary, called on the annulment of all postal votes. [Anna Orosz](/wiki/Anna_Orosz "Anna Orosz") of the Momentum Movement demanded that the government ensure a level playing field for all Hungarian citizens voting abroad. Jobbik politician [Dániel Z. Kárpát](/wiki/D%C3%A1niel_Z._K%C3%A1rp%C3%A1t "Dániel Z. Kárpát") compared the scandal with the infamous ["blue-ballot" election in 1947](/wiki/1947_Hungarian_parliamentary_election "1947 Hungarian parliamentary election"). The Fidesz blamed the United for Hungary for burning the ballot papers, saying "the desperate left-wing is capable of anything despicable". The Fidesz-ally UDMR also condemned the "accusations of election fraud", assuming "an organized provocation" against the ethnic Hungarian minority in Romania.[[49]](#cite_note-targumures-58) The National Election Commission (NVB) announced on 3 April it will not investigate the issue of the discarded and burned postal ballots, because the election law only covers the territory of Hungary. The [Curia of Hungary](/wiki/Curia_of_Hungary "Curia of Hungary") upheld the NVB's decision on 10 April 2022.[[53]](#cite_note-62)

The ruling Fidesz acquired 93.91 percent of the mail votes, which accounted for 8% of all votes cast on the party's national list. This meant an extra mandate for Fidesz. The United for Hungary and the Our Homeland gained only 4.1 and 1.06 percent, respectively.[[54]](#cite_note-63)

### International responses

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2022_Hungarian_parliamentary_election&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: International responses")]

Heads of government and heads of state congratulating Orbán on his victory included Russian president [Vladimir Putin](/wiki/Vladimir_Putin "Vladimir Putin"),[[55]](#cite_note-64) Czech president [Miloš Zeman](/wiki/Milo%C5%A1_Zeman "Miloš Zeman"), Slovenian prime minister [Janez Janša](/wiki/Janez_Jan%C5%A1a "Janez Janša"),[[56]](#cite_note-65) Croatian prime minister [Andrej Plenković](/wiki/Andrej_Plenkovi%C4%87 "Andrej Plenković"),[[57]](#cite_note-66) Kazakh president [Kassym-Jomart Tokayev](/wiki/Kassym-Jomart_Tokayev "Kassym-Jomart Tokayev"),[[58]](#cite_note-67) Indian prime minister [Narendra Modi](/wiki/Narendra_Modi "Narendra Modi"), Albanian prime minister [Edi Rama](/wiki/Edi_Rama "Edi Rama"),[[59]](#cite_note-these-68) Egyptian president [Abdel Fattah el-Sisi](/wiki/Abdel_Fattah_el-Sisi "Abdel Fattah el-Sisi"),[[60]](#cite_note-69) Israeli president [Isaac Herzog](/wiki/Isaac_Herzog "Isaac Herzog"),[[61]](#cite_note-70) Turkish president [Recep Tayyip Erdoğan](/wiki/Recep_Tayyip_Erdo%C4%9Fan "Recep Tayyip Erdoğan"),[[62]](#cite_note-71) Bulgarian president [Rumen Radev](/wiki/Rumen_Radev "Rumen Radev"),[[63]](#cite_note-72) Kyrgyz president [Sadyr Japarov](/wiki/Sadyr_Japarov "Sadyr Japarov"),[[64]](#cite_note-:6-73) Belarusian president [Alexander Lukashenko](/wiki/Alexander_Lukashenko "Alexander Lukashenko"),[[65]](#cite_note-74) Serbian president [Aleksandar Vučić](/wiki/Aleksandar_Vu%C4%8Di%C4%87 "Aleksandar Vučić"),[[64]](#cite_note-:6-73) Slovak prime minister [Eduard Heger](/wiki/Eduard_Heger "Eduard Heger"),[[66]](#cite_note-75) Chinese premier [Li Keqiang](/wiki/Li_Keqiang "Li Keqiang"),[[64]](#cite_note-:6-73) Austrian chancellor [Karl Nehammer](/wiki/Karl_Nehammer "Karl Nehammer"), and Czech prime minister [Petr Fiala](/wiki/Petr_Fiala "Petr Fiala").[[67]](#cite_note-Telex.hu-76)

Other politicians and political parties to congratulate Orbán included [Matteo Salvini](/wiki/Matteo_Salvini "Matteo Salvini"), leader of Italy's [Northern League](/wiki/Lega_Nord "Lega Nord") and former deputy prime minister,[[68]](#cite_note-77) [Hunor Kelemen](/wiki/Hunor_Kelemen "Hunor Kelemen"), leader of Romania's [UDMR](/wiki/Democratic_Alliance_of_Hungarians_in_Romania "Democratic Alliance of Hungarians in Romania") and deputy prime minister,[[69]](#cite_note-78) [Nigel Farage](/wiki/Nigel_Farage "Nigel Farage"), former leader of United Kingdom's [Brexit Party](/wiki/Reform_UK "Reform UK"),[[70]](#cite_note-79) former Slovak prime minister [Robert Fico](/wiki/Robert_Fico "Robert Fico"),[[59]](#cite_note-these-68) former U.S. president [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump"),[[71]](#cite_note-80) [Marine Le Pen](/wiki/Marine_Le_Pen "Marine Le Pen"), leader of France's [National Rally](/wiki/National_Rally "National Rally"),[[72]](#cite_note-81) Dutch [Party for Freedom](/wiki/Party_for_Freedom "Party for Freedom") politician [Geert Wilders](/wiki/Geert_Wilders "Geert Wilders"), [Milorad Dodik](/wiki/Milorad_Dodik "Milorad Dodik"), the Serb member of the [Presidency of Bosnia and Herzegovina](/wiki/Presidency_of_Bosnia_and_Herzegovina "Presidency of Bosnia and Herzegovina"),[[67]](#cite_note-Telex.hu-76) and the Spanish political party [Vox](/wiki/Vox_(political_party) "Vox (political party)").[[73]](#cite_note-82) Reflecting ongoing tensions over Russian sanctions, Polish prime minister [Mateusz Morawiecki](/wiki/Mateusz_Morawiecki "Mateusz Morawiecki") was slightly more reserved but said that "regardless of [Poland's] relations with Hungary, we must note that the Fidesz-Christian Democrat alliance won its fourth consecutive election with the best possible results."[[59]](#cite_note-these-68)

The [European Council](/wiki/European_Council "European Council") president [Charles Michel](/wiki/Charles_Michel "Charles Michel") congratulated Orbán, while the [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO") Secretary General [Jens Stoltenberg](/wiki/Jens_Stoltenberg "Jens Stoltenberg") expressed "acknowledgment" of the results.[[64]](#cite_note-:6-73) Two days after the election, the European Commission president [Ursula von der Leyen](/wiki/Ursula_von_der_Leyen "Ursula von der Leyen") announced that the commission would begin cutting funding to Hungary over rule-of-law concerns.[[74]](#cite_note-83) Hungarian government officials criticized the move as an attempt to "punish Hungarian voters".[[75]](#cite_note-84)
